speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did, I felt terribly sorry. I was afraid they're going to kill him there. So one afternoon, I took guts and I went upstairs to Schindler. And that was something, to go upstairs to Schindler. They didn't want to let me in, but I said, Schindler called me. He never called me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into his office. He was sitting, and he looked up at me. He said, what are you doing here? Aren't you by work-- working? I said, yes, Herr Director. We used to call him Herr Director. I have something to ask you, a question. He didn't answer me a word. He didn't look at me. He didn't-- said nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I have a friend who was left after my whole family died. I have a friend. He's so good to me, but he's in Plaszow. He's afraid that they're going to kill him. I beg you, bring him down here to us. I couldn't believe it that I said that. That slipped out of my mouth. He didn't answer me a word, nothing. He just looked up to me with his blue eyes, didn't say a word. Go back to work. All right. I left him, and I went back to work. And I thought, forget it. He will not do it. But it didn't take long. Take a few days, and he came down. They brought him to our camp. Sam came down. You see, he did it for me, and he didn't tell me he would do it. That's why I am telling you that I-- that was my savior. I loved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? He was with us in camp, Sam. It was better, you know, to have somebody to take care of me, to say a good word, you know, or bring me something. And that's how we went there until it came that, that they're going to clean up the camp. They're going to send us away.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Schindler, that time, they took him to Berlin because he did a lot on the black market, Schindler, you know. And somebody tell the high and mighty in Berlin, and they put him to prison. For a few days, they kept him. He was not long there because he had a lot, a lot of push around, you know. They let him out. But when did they let him out, then they took us by mistake to Auschwitz, 300 women. Over 300 women, they were supposed to send to Brinnlitz, to Schindler's factory, to another factory. They took us to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We got scared because Auschwitz was the end for us. We didn't know what's going to be, what will happen. Meantime, we were working, building barracks, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you together with Sam in Plaszow before this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow before they sent--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Schindler barracks together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were before and then when I was going to Schindler, he was still left. Then they sent us up. When it came the order to send us to Brinnlitz, they sent us up to Plaszow. We were waiting for that they're going to send us to Brinnlitz. And meantime, he was also up in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Altogether, how much time did you spend in Plaszow--

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. Maybe a few-- two, three months. But then, all of a sudden came the order to send the men away. They are going away. They sent Sam to Mauthausen, and we were waiting to go to Brinnlitz. And they took Sam to the wagons already to go to Mauthausen. It was a terrible, hot day. It was July. It was so hot, and Plaszow was hot. The men were dying in the wagons waiting, all ready to go. Schindler came and he gave an order to spread cold water on the roofs of the wagons to make it cooler, cooler. To hear something like that as such a Nazi, he came to help the Jewish people to get a little cold water, you know. And then my husband left me. He went to, to Mauthausen. And a few days later, they took us to Auschwitz. But Schindler wasn't already with us. He was already in Berlin in prison when they took us to Auschwitz. You know from the picture what happened. One night, he came, and he brought with him jewelry and diamonds and gold, and he bought us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hear your story. Could you describe the journey from Plaszow to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, this I never forget. They took us with the thinking that we are going to Czechoslovakia, to Brinnlitz. And we all were happy. We're going to Schindler. We're going to Schindler to work. We're going. And all of a sudden, in the night, we came over to a camp, and we saw the oven. The smoke came out from the ovens, you know. They were burning. So we knew that we are in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had heard of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard of it before you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We heard before. We heard. And, and they took us there, and we didn't know what happened because we were not supposed to go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the doors opened off the cars, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we saw? Fire coming out from the chimneys. And we knew this  are the chimneys where they're burning people, the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it smell like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It smells-- how can I explain you how it smells? It was choking. So we start to cry, start to hug each other, and where are we? What's going to be? What's going to be? But all right, they took us to some shower that time. They gave us a shower. And we thought that we are going already to the oven, not to a shower. We knew-- you remember from the picture the women were going in a room, and they thought that's a gas chamber. We were-- and then, all of a sudden, water came down. It was a shower. They gave us a shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to have your hair cut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they shaved off our heads. They shaved off my head, and I was happy. I was so happy they shaved off my head because the lice were killing me. And they shaved it off. They, they put some, some-- you know, that, that-- I don't know the name of it. Naphtha, they call it in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Disinfectant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Disinfect us, yeah, disinfect. And they shaved us all around. Men, can you imagine? Can you imagine a woman, and a man takes care of such a thing? That, that was degrading. We felt all degrading. We were low, low, low. And that's all. And then we went to the barracks, and they started to push us to work. We worked a few weeks there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a magazine to take some clothes. I took some rags, clothes. I took some rags, and I took some shoes. But I didn't have shoes. I had the, the Dutch, Dutch wooden shoes, you know, what they wearing in Holland. And that was winter already. That was winter-- was snow on the ground and high snow, very high snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went to work, running because they was yelling, oh, oh, oh, fast, fast, fast. So running, I lost a shoe. So I took the other shoe. I throw it away also because how can I run in one shoe? You can break your neck. And I was working barefoot. Doing what kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Building barracks, carrying wooden, you know, wood for the barracks. There was hard work, very hard work. And then, Schindler came, as you know the story. We didn't know that he is in Auschwitz. But the girls from Auschwitz, they came over, and there were windows around that room. We had the windows. And they knocked to the window, and they said to us, Schindler is here. Schindler is here because Schindler-- everybody knew Schindler. Who's with Schindler is saved. So we knew Schindler is here. It's OK. So we are saved. And then, he bought us out for money, for diamonds, for jewelry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take from when you heard he was in the camp to when you got out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long, a few days, a few days because then he brought the list with him because he wanted his women. He didn't want other women from Auschwitz. He wanted everyone who worked for him and was on the list-- that one he wanted. So when he was bargaining to buy us out, he said, I give you 300 younger girls, good workers. He said, I don't want it. I don't want nobody. I wanted my girls. He has the list, he said. He has 300 women, everyone by name. So the day when they put us on the platz, on the plaza, and they were calling us by names, they called out by names, I remember, no numbers. We didn't have numbers. We were not numbered. Schindler's were never not numbered.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they took us to the train, and they took us to Brinnlitz. We came to Brinnlitz. Schindler was waiting for us, you know. Soup was cooked with meat, hot soup. And he looked at us. I was without hair. I didn't have hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, oh, he said, what they did to you, what they did to you. You know, he was sorry. He was very sorry. Then he gave us food. He put us to sleep in a barrack. And we were saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were his barracks like compared to the ones in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, much better, much better-- built better, you know, better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we had a blanket. We had a pillow-- not a pillow but a piece of something. But we were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz, did you have any blanket or pillow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, I don't remember to have a blanket. Yeah, I think we, we had some. Somebody had some, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had a bad time in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you describe a typical day in Auschwitz while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Typical-- well, every day was the same. In the morning, it was dark, still night. They called you out, and they counted us. You could stay in the frosting cold-- you could stay two hours until they count every one. And when they counted us, they took us to work. We had to go to work, and we were working. Then in the evening, they brought us back to the barrack, and we got a, a hot soup. It was soup. It was like water, potatoes, something. But we were happy. It was hot. And we went to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the morale?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very low, very low.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and the other girls talk about in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- what I was talking-- I tell you the truth-- very funny. I was dreaming about meatballs, meatballs in the gravy with brown kasha. You know what's kasha? I loved it. I loved it. Now, I can't eat, but that time, I was crazy. That's what I was talking. I said, God, if I could have now [LAUGHS] plates with that, would I eat. And they said, don't mention it. Don't mention. And they were-- they couldn't talk about food. But everyone was hungry. [LAUGHS] But people dreaming about a good chicken, a good challah like Mama used to bake.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, what, what could you do? There were no more movies, no concerts, no entertainment. You had to kill your time with something, no? So what was the journey from Auschwitz to Brinnlitz like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, from Auschwitz to Brinnlitz, that was a joyful-- was so-- we were so happy. We knew that we are going to Oskar Schindler. It was fantastic. We came there. It was already evening. He was walking, waiting for us, waiting. And they brought us to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he-- all of you here? All of you here? Oh, oh, Edith, or the-- by name he knew us. He knew me by name. He was like a father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that was our, our trip to Brinnlitz. When was this? Do you know when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wintertime-- that was, that was end of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in, in Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brinnlitz, where was I? Oh, I was working by ammunition. There were big factory, ammunition, they were making for the war. They were sending this to the front, and I was working by a very big machine. That machine was-- we used to put in powder. It was such a-- I always forget. I even-- on television, I forgot also the name of that, what they put in the powder, and I am supposed to close it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the machine was closing it. And I stepped with my foot. I closed it, you know. But I always forgot to put the powder in, but I close it, you know. I was saboteuring. I didn't care. What do I care?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, Schindler knew about that. Schindler told that after the war, he told me because he was at my wedding after the war. So he told me, [GERMAN]. I know what you were doing. But I didn't say nothing. If he would have said one word, [POINTING] they would kill me on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there in the ammunition factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until May 5, May 5 when they liberated us, the Russian. The Russian came in. They liberate our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that day was-- I got drunk. That was the day when I got drunk. But we had like a mother in our camp. She was watching over us, you know, the younger girls. She was watching. She was an older lady, a very fine woman. She was always watching us. But when the Russian came in-- they liberate our camp-- the Russians were very wild. Soldiers were very wild. So they were raping girls. They, they wanted to go into our camp where we were sleeping. So she closed the door, closed the door-- didn't let nobody go in to us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember her name. It was an older lady. She was limping a little bit. She was such a wonderful-- so she saved us from the wild Indians, like they say. But all right, second day, the men, our men from the camp, our men, they took care. They watched over us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the Russian came over-- because they brought us a, a live cow to the camp. And the Jewish people, the butchers and butchers and this one, they killed the cows, and they cooked a dinner. We got a fantastic dinner that time, a wonderful dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what was the joke on us that they grabbed two Germans, you know, and they hanged them on the ceiling. And the Germans were hanging over us, hanging, you know, dead already but hanging. But we were eating. We didn't lost our appetite. We were eating, looking at them hanging. It was a nice picture because they were terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that, that was a very beautiful day, [LAUGHS] liberation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A very sad moment was when Schindler came over, and he called everybody to a room, to the big hall. And he told us, listen, the war is over. You are saved. He told us, you're safe. The war is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody started to cry, to hug, to kiss, you know. Then we started to think about how to save Schindler because if the Russians would grab him, he would be finished because he-- they'd call him a traitor. He was a traitor, a traitor to Czechoslovakia, a traitor even to the Germans. They don't mind, but he was a traitor. They wanted to kill him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a bunch of our boys, they-- you saw on, on television that-- how they saved him. They took him out, and they went with him to a border. They saved him. Then he came in Germany. He came to us there in Ginsburg, where we were. And he came there. We gave him an apartment. He got an apartment. He got food, everything. We'll come back to that. Let's go back to where you went after--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you saw Schindler off at the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: After, after Auschwitz, when they let us out, we didn't know where to run. But everybody ran to his city.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Brinnlitz or Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: After finish of the war, from Brinnlitz, we took the train, and we went back to Cracow. We came to Cracow-- I came to Cracow alone and was looking for some family, for, for relatives, for family, but I didn't found nobody. There was nobody saved, nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So walking on the street, a woman started to yell from across the street. She started to cry, and she started to call me by name. And I didn't know who she is. It was an older lady-- familiar face. It was my mother's girlfriend. She had a bakery there-- beautiful. She called my name. I said, in Polish, I said, who are you? She says, don't you recognize me? I am your mother's friend. You come on. You come with me. She took me her house, closed me in a room because she also was afraid of the Russians. Because they were running around in Cracow. Clothed me, fit me, dressed me up, and gave me a hot bath. And that was the most beautiful day of my life. Then I didn't have nobody, and I didn't know where somebody is. I didn't know that Sam is alive. I didn't know for a year that he's alive. But he, being in Germany, liberated, he was walking on the street. And he met my girlfriend on the street who came from Brinnlitz there to Linz. She saw him on the street, and she said to him, Sam, what are you doing here? He said, I was just liberated from the American Army, because the Americans were in Germany. So she said, oh, yes, I'm going to tell you something to shock you. She said, do you know that Edith is in Cracow? She went from Brinnlitz to Cracow, and she is in Cracow. He didn't ask her a word where, what, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: As he was staying on the street, he went the the, to the bahnhof, to the train station, took a train, and came to Cracow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he started to ask around, and they told him where I am, who took me, and he came over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother's girlfriend was scared. Who's he? Who's he? She didn't want to let him in. So he said, I am boyfriend of Edith. I love her very much, and I am so happy that she's saved. And he told her the whole story, so she opened the door and let me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, from eating good food, you know, being skinny, I got swollen. I swell up. I swell up. My hands was like that. You put, you put a finger, a hole-- you know, that was the reaction of the food. You swell up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he wasn't shocked, no, nothing. He said, you're not going to stay another day here. We are going back. Where we are going? Where the Americans are, to Germany. Then we went to Regensburg, to Germany. There we married a year later. What day did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24th of February.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Regensburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were staying from May until, I think, February next year. We went to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like before you went to the States, in Regensburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. I was working with the American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was [INAUDIBLE] office. I was taking care of our office. People come. I used to register them, used to give out cards for milk or food to help out. They came-- some of them, they came with, with little children, but they ran away from some place. Some Russian Jews came over there also. They ran away from some place. So we were taking care of the people there. We had an office.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the post-war community like where you were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Post-war community Cracow, in Cracow, you mean, or Berlin?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Regensburg, where you were living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Regensburg-- they were very unhappy Germans because we took away the apartments. We threw them out on the street, and we took over, the Jewish people. And, and that's-- they were very unhappy, but they didn't say a word. They were sitting quiet because they were afraid to say something. We had a nice social life. There was a lot of our people came from the camps. We made a community, Jewish community. We had a synagogue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Americans were helping with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide you wanted to emigrate to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they was calling the people. The American Army was calling to the people wherever somebody wants to go. So when they asked me, I was called by a high rank officer to ask where I want to go. He was registering. I said, I tell you the whole truth. I don't know where to go. I don't know where to go. He said, so don't worry. You are going to the United States. This is the best place in the world. You're going to be peaceful.
